## [03:02 - 06:02]

So a few things, are you clear on your goals? Are you clear on what you want? Are you clear on why you want what you want? Are you hungry to achieve those goals? Are you coachable and willing to learn? I'll tell you this, nobody wants to work with somebody with a big ego and it's not coachable. Okay, even if you're very, very good, it's not the type of guy people want to work with. So are you coachable or you willing to learn? Even if you have like crazy experience, be coachable and open minded. Okay, it's a huge turn off if you're not. Are you clear on your income goals? Do you know exactly what you want? Do you know why you want that? They're going to be looking as well if your income goal aligns with what they can provide. Okay, so it can be a red flag if it's way too low. If you're like, oh, it's just 5k a month. There's also a red flag if you say 100k a month and like realistically this is a 20 to $30,000 position. Okay, now there's nothing wrong with saying, look, I want to be a millionaire. I eventually want to make $100,000 a month if not more, but I know where I'm at in the process and I have to respect and make sure I do things in sequence. And right now in sequence, the right next step for me would be to do, you know, have this position, close in this industry, become great at this skill, and I'd be extremely happy with an income of $15,000 to $25,000. Now, that's a way in which you can state you have these huge goals, but also know that you're self-aware and realistic and that it aligns with the position. And you guys should know, at least if you go through us, you should pretty much know what the compensation expectation is because we know and we can tell you beforehand. Now, if you're in the middle of a career transition, you need to be able to very, very clearly communicate why you're transitioning. Or maybe you're in real estate sales or a different industry, time share sales. Well, why are you getting out of that and getting in the high ticket full-time? ## [06:03 - 09:04]

that's going to be a reflection of how you show up on a sales call, okay? And it can be unfortunate because sometimes people for these interviews, they can get way more nervous than maybe they'd even get on a sales call, but you kind of got to get over that because if you're going to get nervous with me on an interview, well, how are you going to react in a very high pressure situation with a prospect? So have normal, great rapport building, social skills, you know, just be really present in the moment is key. And then of course the final thing is track record. So if you sold before, be prepared to share that track record. And it's best done if you can know specific timeframes in which you worked for the company. Units and revenues produced. So one of the things that teach the clients is to really dig in. And if you say you produced, you know, if you've had tremendous success and you've done X and Y and Z to ask them what their closing rate was, ask them how many units they produced in X this month, that month, and really kind of chunk down, so to speak, to see, you know, are you bullshitting or are you telling the truth? Right? Because as we chunk more down into the specifics and we go month by month and find out your units and your revenue produced and all of that stuff, it's going to be pretty obvious of whether you were kind of bullshitting or you were telling the truth. Now that's not to say you got to have a spreadsheet open and know your closing rate for the exact, you know, each month, exact month for the last 12 months like memorized, you should have a good idea and you should be able to explain your track record with specifics, with units and revenue produced fairly easily. You should also know how your track record compared to everybody else.
